findings.title: No concrete authentication/authorization implementation task exists
findings.description: Decision point dp-5 identifies the need for authentication (JWT or mTLS/OAuth2) across all services, but no task or subtask actually implements authentication middleware, token validation, or an identity provider integration. All API endpoints (Equipment Catalog, RMS, Finance, Vetting, Social Media Engine, Morgan web chat) appear to be deployed without any auth gates. The rate limiter in task 2005 uses IP/tenant header, but the X-Tenant-ID header is not authenticated, meaning anyone can spoof it. This leaves every service endpoint open to unauthenticated access.
findings.recommendation: Add a dedicated task to implement an authentication layer: deploy an OAuth2/OIDC provider (e.g., Keycloak, Auth0, or Zitadel), implement JWT validation middleware in each backend service (Rust, Go, Node.js), create service account tokens for inter-service calls, and integrate user authentication in the Next.js frontend. This should be a dependency for all service tasks.

findings.title: No RBAC/permissions model for application-level authorization
findings.description: While Kubernetes RBAC is addressed in tasks 1001 and 10001, there is no application-level authorization model. The Finance service (invoices, payments, payroll), RMS (opportunities, projects, inventory), and Vetting service all have sensitive endpoints with no role-based access control. Any authenticated user could potentially access any endpoint including payroll records, financial reports, and vetting results.
findings.recommendation: Define an application-level RBAC model with roles (admin, sales, operations, finance, viewer) and implement authorization middleware in each service that checks the user's role/permissions from their JWT claims against the required permissions for each endpoint.

findings.title: No encryption at rest for sensitive database fields
findings.description: The PostgreSQL database stores highly sensitive data including customer PII (names, emails, phones in vetting and CRM), financial data (invoices, payments, payroll with gross/net amounts), credit check results, and Stripe payment IDs. No task addresses column-level encryption or transparent data encryption (TDE) for the PostgreSQL cluster. The database credentials are stored in Kubernetes secrets, but the data itself is unencrypted at rest on disk.
findings.recommendation: Enable PostgreSQL TDE via the CloudNative-PG operator's encryption settings, or implement application-level encryption for PII columns (customer emails, phone numbers, addresses, credit data) using a key management approach. Ensure the encryption keys are stored separately from the data (e.g., in a KMS or Vault).

findings.title: PII handling and GDPR compliance lacks implementation detail
findings.description: Decision point dp-10 identifies GDPR compliance needs, and soft-delete is mentioned in several schemas (deleted_at columns), but there is no task for implementing data export (right of access), data portability, consent management, or the scheduled hard-deletion process. The vetting service stores third-party credit data and business information that may have additional regulatory requirements. No data classification or data flow mapping task exists.
findings.recommendation: Add a dedicated task for GDPR compliance: implement a data export API endpoint per service, create a scheduled job for hard-deleting records past retention period, implement consent tracking, create a data classification document mapping PII fields across all schemas, and ensure audit logging covers all PII access.

findings.title: Insufficient input validation strategy across services
findings.description: While some services mention serde validation (Rust) or Effect Schema (Node.js), the validation depth is inconsistent. The Equipment Catalog's equipment_checkout endpoint (2004) accepts customer_info with name/email/phone without sanitization guidance. The RMS service's barcode scanning endpoint (3005) accepts arbitrary barcode strings. The Social Media Engine's upload endpoint (6011) handles file uploads without explicit content-type validation beyond MIME type. SQL injection protection relies entirely on sqlx parameterized queries but this is not explicitly verified as a requirement.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit input validation requirements to each endpoint task: define max field lengths, regex patterns for emails/phones, barcode format validation, and file upload restrictions (max size, allowed MIME types, magic byte verification). Require parameterized queries in code review criteria. Add a fuzzing/property-testing subtask for critical input paths.

findings.title: Secrets stored as plaintext Kubernetes Secrets with placeholder values in manifests
findings.description: Task 1006 creates secrets with 'placeholder values (to be filled by operators)' committed as manifests under infra/secrets/. Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted. Committing even placeholder secret manifests to a repo sets a pattern where actual values could accidentally be committed. There is no mention of SealedSecrets, SOPS, or external secrets operators for the initial bootstrap, only discussed as a decision point (dp-15) in task 10.
findings.recommendation: Use SealedSecrets or External Secrets Operator from day one (not just in production hardening). Never commit secret manifests with placeholder values to source control. Instead, create a secret provisioning script or use a GitOps-compatible secret management approach. Move dp-15 decision earlier in the timeline.

findings.title: ConfigMap contains database connection string templates that could leak topology
findings.description: Task 1007 creates a ConfigMap 'sigma1-infra-endpoints' with database hosts, ports, and service names that is replicated across multiple namespaces. While passwords are excluded, the connection string templates reveal internal service topology. Any pod in any namespace with this ConfigMap can discover all infrastructure endpoints, violating least-privilege information access.
findings.recommendation: Instead of a single monolithic ConfigMap replicated everywhere, create per-service ConfigMaps containing only the endpoints that specific service needs. Use Kubernetes RBAC to restrict ConfigMap access to only the intended service accounts.

findings.title: Stripe webhook signature verification is good, but no CSRF protection on state-changing endpoints
findings.description: The Stripe webhook (4005) correctly implements signature verification. However, state-changing endpoints across services (POST, PUT, DELETE on invoices, payments, opportunities, projects, drafts) have no mention of CSRF protection. The web chat WebSocket endpoint (7004) and social media approval links (6010) generated via Signal are particularly vulnerable to CSRF if session cookies are used.
findings.recommendation: If cookie-based sessions are used, implement CSRF tokens on all state-changing endpoints. If using Bearer token authentication only, CSRF is mitigated but ensure tokens are never stored in cookies. Document the anti-CSRF strategy. For the Signal approval links, use single-use tokens with short TTLs.

findings.title: No dependency auditing or container image scanning tasks
findings.description: No task addresses dependency vulnerability scanning (cargo audit, npm audit, govulncheck), container image scanning, or SBOM generation. The plan uses multiple languages (Rust, Go, Node.js) each with their own dependency ecosystems, increasing the attack surface. Container base images (rust:1.75-slim, debian:bookworm-slim) are specified but not scanned.
findings.recommendation: Add a CI/CD task that runs cargo audit, npm audit/snyk, and govulncheck as part of every build. Integrate Trivy or Grype for container image scanning. Pin all dependency versions in lock files. Use distroless or scratch-based final container images where possible instead of debian:bookworm-slim.

findings.title: Database connections may not use TLS
findings.description: PostgreSQL connection strings reference sqlx with tls-rustls feature in Rust services, but the actual PostgreSQL cluster configuration in task 1002 does not mention enabling TLS/SSL on the server side. Similarly, Redis in task 1003 does not mention TLS configuration. Internal traffic between services and databases traverses the cluster network without encryption unless explicitly configured.
findings.recommendation: Configure TLS on the CloudNative-PG cluster using operator-managed certificates. Enable TLS on Redis via the operator CR. Require all client connections to use TLS (sslmode=verify-full for PostgreSQL, TLS for Redis). This is especially important if network policies have gaps or in multi-tenant cluster environments.

findings.title: S3/R2 bucket CORS and access configuration may be overly permissive
findings.description: Task 1004 configures CORS on S3/R2 buckets to 'allow GET from the web domain' and mentions public bucket URLs for CDN-backed reads. If misconfigured, product images and event photos could be publicly accessible without any access control. The task does not mention bucket policies to restrict listing or prevent unauthorized uploads.
findings.recommendation: Configure bucket policies to deny public listing (ListBucket). Use pre-signed URLs for uploads instead of direct write access. Restrict CORS origins to specific domains. Consider using Cloudflare Workers or signed URLs for read access to prevent hotlinking. Audit bucket ACLs as part of security testing.

findings.title: Application-level security event logging is insufficient
findings.description: While Kubernetes audit logging (task 10004) and PostgreSQL audit logging (task 10005) are addressed, application-level security events are not systematically logged. Authentication failures, authorization denials, rate limit violations, input validation failures, and suspicious activity patterns are not mentioned as structured log events. The Prometheus metrics (task 2006) track request counts but not security-specific events.
findings.recommendation: Define a security event taxonomy and add structured security logging to all services: log authentication failures, authorization denials, rate limit triggers, input validation failures, and anomalous request patterns. Include correlation IDs for cross-service tracing. Feed these logs into the alerting system (task 18) with rules for brute force detection, credential stuffing, and API abuse.

findings.title: Morgan agent web chat has no abuse prevention beyond basic rate limiting
findings.description: The Morgan web chat endpoint (7004) creates anonymous sessions and connects to an AI agent backed by GPT-5.4-pro. Rate limiting is mentioned but there's no protection against prompt injection, conversation hijacking, or abuse of the AI agent to extract internal system information. The agent has MCP tool access to all backend services (catalog, RMS, finance, vetting, social media), making it a high-value target.
findings.recommendation: Implement prompt injection defenses in the Morgan agent (input sanitization, output filtering, system prompt hardening). Add per-session and per-IP conversation limits. Implement tool-call authorization so anonymous web chat users cannot trigger sensitive operations (credit checks, invoice creation, payment processing) without authentication. Add monitoring for anomalous tool usage patterns.

findings.title: Signal messaging integration stores sensitive business data in third-party messaging platform
findings.description: The Signal integration (tasks 1005, 7002, 6010) is used for both customer communications and internal approval workflows. Quotes, vetting results, and social media content previews are sent via Signal. While Signal is end-to-end encrypted, the Signal-CLI rest API (task 1005) stores registration state on a PVC, and the approval workflow (6010) sends draft content including image URLs over Signal.
findings.recommendation: Minimize sensitive data sent via Signal — send notification links rather than full content. Ensure the Signal-CLI data PVC is encrypted at rest. Implement message retention policies. Consider the Signal-CLI rest API as a high-security component and restrict network access to it. Do not include internal URLs or API tokens in Signal messages.

findings.title: Container images not specified from trusted registries with digest pinning
findings.description: Task 1005 references 'bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api' which is a community Docker Hub image. Other tasks specify base images by tag (rust:1.75-slim, debian:bookworm-slim) without SHA256 digest pinning. This creates supply chain risk from image tampering or tag mutation.
findings.recommendation: Pin all base images by SHA256 digest. For third-party images like signal-cli-rest-api, either fork and build internally, or use a container registry mirror with vulnerability scanning. Implement image admission policies (e.g., Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) to enforce trusted registries and prevent unsigned images.

findings.title: Pod security standards not explicitly enforced
findings.description: No task mentions Kubernetes Pod Security Standards (PSS) or Pod Security Admission (PSA). Without these, containers may run as root, mount host paths, or use privileged capabilities. The signal-cli-rest-api container in particular may require elevated permissions.
findings.recommendation: Apply Pod Security Standards at the namespace level using Pod Security Admission labels (enforce: restricted or baseline). Explicitly configure securityContext on all containers: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem where possible, drop all capabilities, and set runAsUser/runAsGroup. Document exceptions (e.g., signal-cli if it requires specific permissions).

findings.title: No incident response or security alerting beyond SLO violations
findings.description: Task 18 defines alerting for SLO violations and infrastructure events, but there are no security-specific alerts: no alerting on failed authentication spikes, RBAC violations, secret access anomalies, or suspicious API patterns. Audit logs are collected (tasks 10004, 10005) but not monitored for security events.
findings.recommendation: Add security-specific alert rules: alert on >N failed auth attempts in M minutes, alert on RBAC deny events from unexpected service accounts, alert on secret access from new sources, alert on unusual tool invocation patterns in the Morgan agent. Define an incident response playbook for each alert type.

findings.title: File upload endpoints lack comprehensive security controls
findings.description: The Social Media Engine upload endpoint (6011/6003) accepts multipart file uploads that are stored in S3/R2 and processed by AI services. While 'invalid file types rejected at upload' is mentioned in testing (6012), the implementation details (6003) only mention using 'sharp' for image processing without explicit: max file size limits, content-type verification beyond extension, malware scanning, image bomb/zip bomb protection, or stripping of EXIF metadata that may contain GPS coordinates.
findings.recommendation: Implement: max file size limit (configurable, e.g., 50MB), content-type verification using magic bytes not just extension, image dimension limits to prevent decompression bombs, EXIF metadata stripping (sharp can do this), and consider ClamAV or similar for malware scanning of uploaded files. Process uploads in an isolated context.

strengths: Stripe webhook signature verification is correctly planned with HMAC-SHA256 validation and idempotent event processing via Redis (task 4005)
Network policies with default-deny posture and explicit allow rules are properly planned (task 9005/15)
Kubernetes RBAC is addressed at both bootstrap (task 1001) and production hardening (task 10001) with least-privilege principles
Secret rotation for both database credentials and API keys is explicitly planned with zero-downtime requirements (tasks 10002, 10003)
Soft-delete with audit logging is consistently applied across database schemas for GDPR-aware data management
Rate limiting with fail-open behavior and per-tenant scoping is well-designed in the Equipment Catalog service (task 2005)
Health/readiness probes are planned for all services, enabling proper Kubernetes lifecycle management
The plan includes comprehensive failover and recovery testing (task 9007) which validates HA mechanisms under failure conditions
PostgreSQL audit logging via pgaudit and Redis ACL logging are explicitly planned (task 10005)
summary: The plan has a critical gap in authentication and authorization — no task implements actual auth middleware, identity provider setup, or application-level RBAC, leaving all API endpoints unprotected. Combined with high-severity findings around PII encryption at rest, secret management practices (plaintext manifests in source control), and insufficient input validation for file uploads, the overall security posture requires significant additions before production deployment. The plan does well on infrastructure-layer security (network policies, K8s RBAC, secret rotation, audit logging) but application-layer security is largely absent.
